pattern: Appending a number's binary digits to a running value is the same as shifting the running value left by that number's bit-length and adding it in, so the whole concatenation can be built in one pass without ever materializing a string.
hints: Concatenating binary representations is a shift-and-add operation: shifting the accumulated result left by the new number's bit length, then adding the new number in, mirrors appending its bits.
Track how many bits the current number needs; that count only changes exactly at powers of two.
Keep a running result modulo 1e9+7, and for each i from 1 to n update result = (result << bitLength(i)) + i, taking the modulo every step so it never grows unbounded.

edge_inputs.description: a handful of small numbers spanning a bit-length change
slightly larger n crossing multiple bit-length boundaries
n itself is a power of two, right where bit length increments
n just before a power of two, checking the boundary isn't off by one
n large enough that the modulo must actually engage

stress.description: n near the top of the range to exercise the modulo arithmetic repeatedly
